summary: The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting to deliberate on Zoning Case ZC-2025-001, a proposed MAP amendment from M1 Light Manufacturing to PD Development District for a $50 million apartment project on Smith Road. The trustees voted 2-1 to deny the rezoning request, with Trustees Allen and Hauserman wanting to preserve the M1 industrial zoning for future business development. The board also approved two zoning resolution text amendments regarding residential site development standards and prohibited activities, and held a workshop with the Zoning Commission, BZA, and attorney Kevin Mayloff to discuss potential future zoning changes including PDR lot sizes, setbacks, and PDM business requirements.
decisions: Motion to adopt the agenda passed 3-0
Resolution denying MAP amendment from M1 to PD Development District for property on north side of Smith Road (ZC-2025-001) passed 2-1 (Allen yes, Hauserman yes, Hodges nay)
Resolution R-019-2025 modifying Article 5 Table 5-2 Site Development Standards for Residential Zoning Districts passed 3-0
Resolution R-020-2025 modifying Article 5.03A3 Prohibited Activities passed 3-0
Motion to adjourn at 7:05 p.m. passed 3-0
action_items: Director Langenheim to research and prepare information on pros/cons of eliminating vs. increasing minimum lot sizes for PDR developments
Director Langenheim to research neighboring townships' minimum lot sizes (Miami Township noted at approximately 8,000 sq ft)
Director Langenheim to prepare analysis of how setbacks interact with lot size requirements and negotiation implications
Director Langenheim and Julia to research how to handle PDM business/residential phasing requirements (business built before or concurrent with residential)
Director Langenheim to prepare information on PDM business requirement scenarios, especially related to potential overlay district projects like Eagle's Nest area
key_amounts: $50 million investment proposed for the denied apartment project
$67,000 spent by prior Board of Trustees on the State Route 28 corridor plan
5,000 square foot current minimum lot size for PDR
12,000 square foot new minimum lot size for R5 residential (effective in 30 days)
20 foot side setbacks established for R1 through R3 residential districts
1% income tax paid by apartment residents to the school district (referenced in discussion)
50% minimum open space requirement with 4 units/acre gross density and 8 units/acre net density
35% minimum open space requirement with 3 units/acre gross density and 6 units/acre net density
5-25% floor area proposed business/light industrial requirement for PDM
notable_topics: Denial of rezoning from M1 Light Manufacturing to PD Development District for apartment project on Smith Road
Preservation of M1 industrial zoning for future business and economic development
State Route 28 corridor plan and business development strategy
Residential site development standards modification (Table 5-2) changing lot sizes from 5 to 3 acres
Prohibited activities zoning resolution modification related to property maintenance and House Bill 315
PDR minimum lot size discussion - elimination vs. increase as negotiation tool
PDM business/industrial requirement addition to prevent PDM from being used as higher-density PDR alternative
Side yard setback requirements - proposal to increase to 20 feet with 7-foot minimum per side
Moratorium on high-density single-family home developments
Overlay district protections for downtown area and potential Eagle's Nest development
attendees: Leah Lewis (mentioned as long-time resident present)
Brian (business owner mentioned in M1 district discussion)
notes: The denied project was a high-end apartment development. Trustee Hodges was the lone dissenting vote, expressing support for the project but acknowledging being 'still a little mixed.' The public hearing phase was already closed from the July 22, 2025 meeting; this meeting was solely for trustee deliberation. An application is already on file for a lot across from Eagle's Nest. The workshop portion had no public comment period by design. The moratorium currently in place covers only high-density single-family homes, not commercial or mixed-use projects. There appears to be significant concern about development pressure from Miami Township and Loveland building out.
title: BOT Special 8-7-25
